data.title: Intel Tested Chipmaking Tools From Firm With Sanctioned China Units
data.description:
Intel (INTC, Financials) has tested chipmaking tools this year from ACM Research, a semiconductor equipment maker with deep ties to China and overseas units targeted by U.S. sanctions, according to people familiar with the matter.

TIntel's most sophisticated manufacturing process, termed 14A, is set to start in 2027. The company looked into using wet etch equipment in this process. Reuters couldn't find any proof that Intel violated U.S. laws, and it's not obvious whether the corporation will end up using the tools.

ACM Research, which is located in California, says it doesn't pose a threat to national security and that its U.S. activities are separate from its sanctioned branch in Shanghai. The business added that its American team has sold tools to clients in the U.S. and that it has put in place measures to secure customer data and trade secrets.

China hawks and former U.S. officials stated that the testing makes them worry about possible knowledge transfer, supply chain weakness, and the loss of business for Western equipment suppliers. The problem comes up as Washington relaxes certain limits on chip exports to China and Congress think about a proposal that would stop U.S. chipmakers from using Chinese equipment if they get government money.

Intel did not answer when asked for a remark. As the U.S. works to revive its own semiconductor production, this trend shows that people are paying more attention to supply chain decisions.

data.title: Nvidia Faces Senate Scrutiny Over Trump Approval for China Chip Sales
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) is facing renewed political scrutiny after U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren called for Chief Executive Jensen Huang to testify before Congress over the Trump administration's decision to approve sales of the company's advanced AI chips to China.

Speaking on the Senate floor, Warren urged Huang and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to explain how the policy shift aligns with national security priorities. Her remarks followed President Donald Trump's announcement that his administration would allow exports of Nvidia's H200 chip, the company's second-most advanced artificial intelligence processor, to Chinese customers under a licensing framework.

Warren also asked whether the administration may get in the way of a Justice Department crackdown on H200 chip smuggling on the same day. She warned that the choice may give Beijing more power to snoop and build up its military.

The White House made a clear distinction between illegal smuggling and legal shipments that need to be checked for national security. Nvidia claimed that it would require authorization from the Chinese government to sell chips there, and that these sales will only be a small part of overall advanced semiconductor exports.

Chipmakers and officials are still at odds as Washington tries to reconcile corporate interests with efforts to restrict China's access to cutting-edge technology.

data.title: Crocs: A 17% Earnings Yield Hidden in Plain Sight
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Crocs currently trades around $85 per share for a

market capitalization near $4.7 Billion. With normalized net income over

the last 12 months around $730 Million the shares are currently trading around

6 times earnings or an earnings yield of nearly 17%. This is much cheaper

than the S&P 500 which sits near 31 times earnings or roughly 3.2% earnings

yield. Crocs return most of their excess cash to shareholders in the form

of stock buybacks, just assuming the business remains stable, this is an extremely

attractive investment.

After years of rapid growth which saw the Crocs brand grow from 1.23 billon in

2019 to 3.28 billion in 2024 a calculated annual growth rate (CAGR) of about

21.67%, growth has stalled. In North America sales for the Crocs brand only

grew about 3% and are down around 5% in 2025, indicating a maturing North

American market. This was a conscious decision from management given the tough

2025 year they could have increased promotional activities and lowered prices

to boost the top line but they chose to discounting to preserve their margins

and focus on the long-term health of the brand. Looking forward I expect the

Crocs brand to stabilize and return to growth in the coming years. Management

has said this is a top priority and are confident they can return to profitable

growth in North America in the next few years. Crocs have been able to

diversify their product line outside of the main clog they have shown good

growth in sandals increasing their market share in the 3Q. They are currently

the #1 footwear brand in the U.S on Tiktok which continues to solidify their

brand with younger consumers.

However Internationally it is a different story with

much more room to grow. In 2024 sales grew 12% last year and increased by more

than 10% do far in 2025. In 2024 International sales accounted for about 44%

of total revenue. Leading the growth is China which in 2024 saw 64% yoy

growth, with less than 1% of the market share in China this is a massive growth

opportunity. China currently represents the second largest market currently for

Crocs. I expect the international sales to continue to grow in mid-teens behind

the giant opportunity in China and they only has a 2% or greater market share

in South Korea and the UK leaving much more room for expansion. Even with a

mature North American market the company can still grow long term from

international expansion.

Crocs is a business with very good margins especially for a retailer. In 2024

gross margins were over 58% with operating margins close to 25%. Tariffs in

2025 have put some pressure on Crocs margins. In 2024 51% of Crocs were made in

Vietnam, with the increase in tariifs on Vietnamese-made products and tariffs

on other countries Crocs the parent company expects a 90 million hit to its

bottom line. They have been able to offset this increase in costs by finding 50

million in cost savings in their business. They have also looked at changing

the manufacturing mix however with the tariff rates uncertain it's hard for a

company to predict where it makes the most sense. Margins still remain healthy with

an estimated 24% operating margins for 2025.

Due to the outsourcing business model Crox is a capital

light business. This allows the company to send most earnings back to

shareholders in the form of stock buybacks. The lower the stock trades the more

stock they can buyback increasing shareholder value. In 2024 alone Crox bought

back 7.1% of the entire company, increasing earnings per share and shareholder

value.

This is an investment that doesn't need to grow revenue to

be a good investment. I think they will over time due to international sales

but as long as the business doesn't deteriorate and people buy crocs this is

going to be a solid investment. I think the stock is a buy all the way up to 6

billion which is $109 a share. Crox at $85 a share is a strong buy and has a

clear margin of safety for long term value investors.

Business Overview

Crocs main product are foam clogs, they are big and bulky

and many call them ugly, but they are lightweight, comfy and easy to

wear. Kids will wear their Crocs for all occasions, even during physical

education by pulling the heel strap back and going into sport mode. The

company also owns HeyDude which is more of a lifestyle-oriented shoe brand that

it acquired in 2022. The two brands together had revenue of over $4.1

Billion last year. The company has very healthy margins. Gross margins have

consistently remained above 50%, and operating margins are expected to be

around 24% in 2025 which includes the tariff headwind. Crocs continues to

perform well across most of its businesses, but domestic sales are slowing, and

it has seen some temporary headwinds with HeyDude from over distribution and

excess inventory buildup. In 2024 HeyDude decreased 14% and in the 4Q of 2025

they expect a 20% decrease. This is bad and Crocs certainly overpaid when they

bought HeyDude for 2.5 billion. However it represents just 20% of sales is

still profitable and management expects to be abe to turn it around. My view in

2026 they should be able to stabilize and remain profitable and then look to

return to growth in the HeyDude brand in 2027 and beyond.

The Crocs brand have a very passionate customer base. My mom is a 5th

grade elementary school teacher and say her kids all wear and love their Crocs.

The company sold more than 100 million pairs of shoes last year. Crocs'

customer base is large, cutting across kids, teens, and adults. They have

a high repeat purchase rate helped through partnerships with Disney, Pixar,

McDonald's, and the NFL. Crocs capitalize on personalization which starts with

the Crocs themselves and then you can add special Jibbitz that attach into the

holes of the crocs again adding extra customization. The Jibbitz adds a high

margin product to the crocs while also increasing personalization. The business

is also expanding its footprint globally, with international revenue now

representing approximately 40% of total revenue and growing much faster than

the U.S. market.

HeyDude has taken the more traditional footwear strategy. Their shoes are

popular for their comfort, versatility and just look like a normal pair of

shoes. They have strong retail partnerships with Amazon, Scheels, and Dick's

Sporting Goods. After years of rapid growth HeyDude was plagued with

excess inventory. They have spent the last few years trying to fix the

inventory problems while increasing their direct-to-consumer business which is

more predictable and have larger margins. The HeyDude business remains profitable,

and the company expects sales to stabilize as they continue to work through inventory

and brings new products back to market.

The company outsources production to third-party manufacturers in Asia,

focusing on design and marketing internally. This creates a capital light

business which allows the company to use the excess cash in reducing their debt

from the HeyDude purchase and buying back shares of stock.

Crox is a company with strong cash flow that can return a

significant amount of capital to shareholders in the form of stock buybacks.

Moats and the 7 Powers

WarrenBuffett often talks about the moat of a company. Every business is like a

castle and in order to protect the castle you need a strong moat to keep

competitors from storming the castle and taking your customers away.

Hamilton Helmers book 7 Powers takes Buffett's castle and moat idea

and breaks it down into seven specific strengths called powers. According to

Helmer, every lasting business needs at least one of these powers. If it

doesn't have one, competitors will eventually wear it down and eat away at its profits.

The more powers a company has the bigger and stronger their moat is.

The seven strategic powers are:

  • Scale economies: producing at lower cost as volume grows
  • Network effects: products that become more valuable as more people use them
  • Counter-positioning: taking a strategy incumbents can't easily copy
  • Switching costs: barriers that make customers hesitate to leave
  • Brand: emotional connection and trust that support pricing power
  • Cornered resource: exclusive access to something valuable
  • Process power: unique ways of operating that are hard to replicate
PowerCrocs' PositionComment
Scale EconomiesModerateCrocs benefits from global reach and established supplier relationships that help keep production and logistics costs low.
Network EffectsNoneEach purchase is independent; there is no user network.
Counter-PositioningStrongCrocs leaned into comfort, personalization and function when competitors were focused on fashion and performance.
Switching CostsNoneConsumers can buy other shoes.
BrandStrongEmotional loyalty, especially among kids and teens, reinforced through personalization.
Cornered ResourceNoneNo exclusive inputs or intellectual property.
Process PowerNoneOperations are efficient but not unique.

Brand (The Strongest Power)

The Crox brand is the company's most important long-term durable advantage. You can buy knock-off foam clogs at Walmart or Amazon for $15. However, millions of consumers choose to spend $50 or more on the Croc.

Crocs wins on anti-fashion comfort and customization, rather than high performance athletics or celebrity athlete sponsorship like Nike or Adidas.

Many companies have tried to copy the Crocs Clog but have yet to penetrate the castle. Skechers and Deckers (HOKA and UGG) have tried to storm the castle but have so far been denied. The Jibbitz add-on charms are a part of this, but the real power is emotional: Kids do not just want a foam shoe. They want Crocs and the personalization that comes with it.

Counter-Positioning

Crocs were successful in no small part because they did the exact opposite of what entrenched players were comfortable with. The footwear industry for decades had been all about sleek, fast, and athletic. Crocs said bloated, clunky, cozy, and personal.Scale Economies Crocs does not manufacture their own shoes but they do benefit from scale economies. The manufacturing process is very capital-efficient at scale. Building 100 million pairs of shoes a year allows them to negotiate favorable pricing for raw materials and logistics that boutique brands cannot. This helps keep their gross margins near 50%.

Conclusion

This is a castle with a strong moat. It is not impossible to breach, of course, with fashion risk and competitive copycats always a factor. But this combination of branding, conter positioning, and scale economies make this a much tougher business to dethrone than it's given credit for.

Financial Review and Valuation

Crocs business has transformed over the past five years. In 2019, it was still a weird niche brand. Today, it's a global company generating over $4 Billion in annual revenue and $1 Billion in Operating Income.

Fiscal YearRevenue ($ B)Operating Income ($ M)Net Income ($ M)
20191.23129119
20244.101,022950

Revenue more than tripled in five years, while operating income and net income both increased by about 700%. That growth came from international expansion, higher direct-to-consumer sales, and strong pricing power.

PeriodRevenue CAGROperating Income CAGRNet Income CAGR
1 Year (20232024)~4 %~1 %~0 %
5 Years (20192024)~26 %~47 %~49 %

Recently however growth has cooled, particularly in the United States, and management expects 2025 to remain soft with a weaker third quarter ahead. This has caused the stock to fall under pressure, but this has presented an opportunity for patient long term investors. In 2025 Crocs reported about $950 million in net income. However, this is inflated by a one-time tax credit. On a normalized basis, net income would have been closer to $730 million, assuming a 20% tax rate. Looking ahead 26% revenue growth is likely a thing of the past however I think mid-single digits is realistic.

Free cash flow remains exceptional. The company generated nearly $1 billion last year, repurchased about $550 million of stock which equaled 4.3M shares or about 7% of company. They also paid down $320 million in debt. Total debt now stands near $2.2 billion, down from more than $3 billion following the HeyDude acquisition.

At $85 a share and net income of 730M Crocs offers an earnings yield of roughly 17% This far exceeds a 4% Treasury bond and allows a large margin of safety if net income decreases a modest amount.

Due to slowing revenue growth and some tariff uncertainty I am quite conservative when valuing Crox. The most I am willing to pay is about 10 times earnings and on top of this I want to discount 2024 earnings due to uncertainties in revenue growth and margin pressure. With 730M in net income in 2024 I will be conservative and use 600M about a 20% drop from last year as my earnings figure. So, I am willing to pay 6B for the business. With 55M shares outstanding I am willing to buy the stock up to $109 a share. With the current stock trading around $85 Crox is a strong buy at these levels.

Growth Scenarios

The goal in investing is not to guess the next quarter but to estimate what a business can earn over time. For Crocs, I will illustrate three scenarios on how the future could unfold: 0%, 5%, and 10% annual net income growth over the next decade. Each assumes that Crocs continues repurchasing about 3% of shares per year, a conservative number at the current stock price but consistent with recent history. As you can see from the chart below the downside is limited even assuming 0% growth and a 5 PE ratio.

ScenarioAssumed P/E3-Year CAGR5-Year CAGR10-Year CAGR
0% growth53.1%0.7%1.1%
1022.1%14.1%8.4%
2053.9%31.0%16.2%
5% growth51.6%4.1%6.1%
1028.1%19.6%13.7%
2061.3%37.4%21.8%
10% growth56.3%9.0%11.0%
1034.0%25.2%18.9%
2068.8%43.8%27.5%

Bear Case: This scenario 0% net income growth. This occurs if HeyDude continues to be a drag on earnings never stabilizing and eventually losing profitability and ultimately being sold off or discontinued. The Crocs brand revenue growth stalls domestically while International growth slows and basically offsets the HeyDude disaster. Even in this scenario the downside is limited because the stock is trading at such a low multiple compared to earnings. If earnings simply hold steady and the market eventually values Crocs at ten times earnings, investors could still see returns near 8% annually over the next decade and over 14% the next 5 years. This is what we call a margin of safety.

Base Case: In my opinion, the 5% growth case is the most realistic. This assumes the Crocs brand continues to be a cash cow. Domestic Crocs sales and profits stabilize while international revenue continues to grow in the low to mid-teens. HeyDude eventually stabilizes revenue and remains profitbale. In that range, total returns could reach 20% annually over five years and about 14% per year over ten years if the market assigns just a 10 times earnings multiple. If the earnings multiple gets close to 20 we would see extremely attratice returns of 61%, 37% and 22% over 3, 5, 10 year periods. Consistent stock buybacks and modest income growth will deliver double-digit returns from the current stock price.

Bull Case: At the higher end, Crocs fires on all cylinders. HeyDude eventually returns to growth. Domestic sales return to single digit growth and international sales continue to lead the charge in the double digits. If Crocs can compound earnings closer to 10% annually and the multiple expands toward 20 times earnings, the outcome becomes exceptional. This combination could deliver returns near 25% annually over five years and close to 19% per year over a decade assuming just a 10 times multiple of earnings.

If the stock remains this cheap, management will likely buy back more than 3% of shares each year, since lower the stock price is makes repurchases more effective. The more shares purchased at these levels the better returns will be for shareholders. Warren Buffett (Trades, Portfolio) looks for investments with a large margin of safety. Crox passes this test trading at only 6 times earnings and returning most excess cash to shareholders in the form of stock buybacks.

Risks

Crocs is certainly not a perfect business and has many risks which is why it sells for only 6 times earnings. North America growth has flattened, and the first six months of 2025 sales are down 5%. The company guided a weak third quarter and 2H in 2025.

Tariffs and shipping costs have risen, squeezing margins given that most manufacturing is done in Asia. It's hard to pass these costs on to consumers in a very competitive footwear industry, these increase in costs will lower crocs operating margins.

Fashion is another big uncertainty. Crocs rode the comfort-wear wave, but fashions can change fast and if clogs go out of fashion or the company can't stay fresh in the eyes of younger buyers, sales growth could slow further.

HeyDude has struggled in recent years. The company expects to stabilize revenues and remain profitable but this is something to keep an eye on in the next few years.

The classic clog also still accounts for most of the profits, meaning there is concentration risk. This is not a replacement product if the clog goes out of favor and consumers stop buying this would be very bad for the company. This is the biggest risk to the company and the stock.

Crocs is still very profitable and cash-generative, with a strong balance sheet and ongoing buybacks giving a margin of safety as the company deals with short-term headwinds.

Final Thoughts

CROX does not have a giant moat but has established a durable brand, counter-positioning and economies of scale advantages. It is a strong earnings generator, growing free cash flow to reduce debt and return capital to shareholders. North America growth has stalled, but international still has plenty of upside.

Trading around 6x earnings, CROX is offering quite a bargain if the business turns around. I don't think Crocs is perfect, but I do not see it as broken either. It is a stable profitable business with a pessimistic valuation. If EPS can grow at a modest rate from modest gincome rowth and buybacks, long-term returns should be compelling. Upside in my opinion could be much higher if they can regain momentum domestically while continue to grow internationally and maintain brand relevance. At $85 a share this is a strong buy and a buy at any price under 6 billion market cap or around $109 a share.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Rise as Market Prices Limited Fed Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. Treasury yields increase as eurozone stocks rise while market pricing points to limited further rate cuts by the Federal Reserve next year. "In our view, the Fed will deliver more than the one cut it has penciled in for next year," says Brown Brothers Harriman's Elias Haddad in a note. U.S. labor demand is weak and upside risks to inflation are not materialising, the global head of markets strategy says. The two-year Treasury yield rises 0.7 basis points to 3.535%; the 10-year yield is up 2.5 basis points at 4.165%; while the 30-year yield rises 3.1 basis points to 4.820%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: King Charles to share personal cancer update in TV message
data.description:
By Catarina Demony

King Charles will share details of his experience with cancer and give an update on his treatment in a personal message broadcast on British television on Friday, Buckingham Palace said.

The monarch will "reflect on his own recovery journey," it added, without going into more detail on the current state of his health almost two years after the first announcement he had been diagnosed with an unspecified form of cancer.

The cancer was detected in tests after a corrective procedure for an enlarged prostate, the palace said in February 2024.

Charles has continued to have treatment and, after some time away from the spotlight, returned to public duties in April this year with a busy schedule of ceremonies, appearances and foreign trips.

The message, pre-recorded in late November in his home, Clarence House, will also focus on the importance of cancer screening, and go on air on Channel 4 at 8 p.m. (2000 GMT), the palace said.

It will be released as part of Channel 4's Stand Up to Cancer campaign in partnership with the charity Cancer Research UK.

Charles' first public appearance following his diagnosis took place at the University College Hospital Macmillan Cancer Centre. The visit also marked his appointment as the new patron of Cancer Research UK.

In a message to attendees at an April reception honouring the work of the cancer charities, Charles spoke about his own experience with the disease, saying it showed him "the very best of humanity".

Leading cancer support charity Maggie's said in July that Charles', and his daughter-in-law Kate's, public battles with cancer, have had a huge impact on fellow sufferers, encouraging them to be open about the difficulties of coping with the illness.

data.title: Hapag-Lloyd Makes More-Than $500 Million Order For Eight Container Ships
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Hapag-Lloyd said it ordered eight container ships from Chinese shipyard CIMC Raffles under a contract valued at more than $500 million.

The German shipping giant said Friday the ships, due for delivery in 2028 and 2029, would be powered by efficient methanol engines.

The vessels will each have a capacity of 4,500 20-foot equivalent units, it said.

Additionally, the company said it reached an agreement on 14 long-term charter contracts. The ships are due be delivered between 2027 and 2029, it said.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

RNS Number : 4134L Brickability Group PLC 12 December 2025

12 December 2025

Brickability Group PLC

(the "Company" or the "Group")

PDMR shareholding

Brickability Group PLC, a leading distributor and provider of specialist products and services to the UK construction industry, has been notified of the following transactions by a PDMR of the Company.

On 11 December 2025 Richard Cosgrove, Managing Director of the Bricks and Building Materials and Importing Divisions, exercised 37,004 options over ordinary shares of 1 penny each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares"), which vested pursuant to an award granted on 27 October 2022 under the Brickability Group PLC Long-Term Inventive Plan. The exercise has been satisfied through the allocation of existing ordinary shares held by the Brickability Employee Benefit Trust and, as such, no new ordinary shares have been issued in connection with the option exercise. 

In order to satisfy the personal tax liabilities and other costs arising on exercise, Mr Cosgrove sold 17,832 Ordinary Shares at a price of 52.60 pence. In accordance with the Company's remuneration policy, Mr Cosgrove will be required to hold the balance for a minimum period of two years from the date of vesting.

Following the transactions outlined above, Richard Cosgrove is beneficially interested in a total of 113,183 Ordinary Shares representing approximately 0.04% of the Company's issued share capital.

About Brickability

Brickability Group PLC is a leading distributor and provider of specialist products and services to the UK construction industry. The business comprises four divisions: Bricks and Building Materials, Importing, Distribution and Contracting. With an agile, de-centralised, capital-light business model, supported by a strong balance sheet, Brickability leverages the skills of its people company-wide to effectively service the complex and evolving needs of the construction industry.

Founded in 1985, the Group has grown organically through product diversification and geographic expansion, as well as through the acquisition of specialist businesses that support its long-term strategy for growth. Today, the Group encompasses a diverse portfolio of market-leading brands and a dedicated team of over 800 skilled professionals, led by a management team with deep-rooted knowledge and experience in the UK and European construction industries.

The Group is committed to building better communities throughout the supply chain and supporting the delivery of sustainable developments that enhance the built environment for future generations, while delivering continuous value for shareholders.

data.title: Venezuelan oil riches will stay mostly theoretical
data.description:
By Yawen Chen

The US seizure of an oil tanker is a big escalation amid calls by Washington for the Latin American state’s leader to go. If regime change happened, Venezuela’s 300 bln barrels of reserves could move oil markets. Yet the path to an output surge looks long, costly and uncertain.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

The U.S. has seized a sanctioned oil tanker off the coast of Venezuela, President Donald Trump said on December 10.

The seizure was the first interdiction of an oil cargo or tanker from Venezuela, which has been under U.S. sanctions since 2019. It came as the U.S. executes a large-scale military buildup in the southern Caribbean and as U.S. President Donald Trump pushes for Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro to step down.

The U.S. is preparing to intercept more ships transporting Venezuelan oil, Reuters reported on December 11 citing six sources familiar with the matter.

Maduro used a rare press conference in September to claim that the U.S. was seeking regime change in his country, citing a naval deployment in the Caribbean.

data.title: Food rations to Sudan to be reduced due to funding shortages, WFP says
data.description:
By Olivia Le Poidevin

Food rations will be reduced for communities facing famine in Sudan from next month due to severe funding shortages, the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) said on Friday.

A war that erupted in 2023 between Sudan's army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) has caused what the U.N. has described as the world's worst humanitarian crisis.

Communities in the Darfur region are facing famine and malnutrition following an 18-month RSF siege around the city of Al-Fashir.

"We will be having to reduce from January to 70% rations for communities that are facing famine, and 50% for those that are at risk of famine," Ross Smith, the WFP Director of Emergency Preparedness and Response told reporters in Geneva, speaking via video link from Rome.

"As of April we will fall off a cliff when it comes to funding," he added.

The U.N. estimates that more than 100,000 people have fled from al-Fashir since its capture by the RSF in late October, with about 15,000 arriving in Tawila, a small nearby town under the control of neutral forces.

The U.N. says 650,000 people are now sheltered there in total, with hundreds of thousands of refugees having fled there during previous fighting from Zam Zam camp in April.

While the WFP is able to provide food to people in Tawila, it requires $700 million to maintain its assistance over the next six months across Sudan.

WFP also raised concern that the humanitarian response was not able to keep pace with needs on the ground in Tawila due to logistical challenges such as negotiation approvals to move between areas as well funding challenges.

"These are families that have endured famine for many months on end and mass atrocities, and are now living in overcrowded conditions with very limited support," Smith said, pointing to very limited available health services and people living in precarious shelters made of straw.

Both the army and RSF have been accused by human rights groups of war crimes, charges they deny.
